summary: This short post covers three topics: a teaser for Spolsky's upcoming column on Test-Driven Development in STQE Magazine, a useful Microsoft DLL database tool, and a book recommendation for Dr. Deming's management philosophy. The Deming discussion is the most substantive, arguing against numeric measurement-based incentive systems. Spolsky highlights two key problems: variability in measurements often reflects systemic issues only management can fix, and individuals will optimize locally for the measured metric at the expense of overall company performance.
key_points: TDD requires writing automated tests first, then writing minimal code to satisfy them, with bugs added to the test suite as they're discovered
Microsoft's DLL Help database catalogs every DLL version shipped across all products, useful for debugging version conflicts
Deming's management philosophy warns against rewarding or punishing employees based on numeric measurements
Measurement variability often stems from broken systems that only management can change, not from individual performance differences
Local optimization of a single metric can harm overall company performance
money_quotes: With TDD, you create an automated test first, and only then write the minimal amount of code that you can get away with to satisfy that test.
You can't improve your team's performance just by picking some numeric measurement and then rewarding or punishing people to optimize it.
The variability in the measurement may be caused by a broken system that only management can change, not by individual performance.
People may optimize locally to improve that one measurement, even at the cost of hurting the performance of the company as a whole.
If you're in a rut constantly trying to figure out how to rejigger your employees' incentive systems, this book will get you out of it.
key_insight: Numeric performance metrics and incentive systems often backfire because they conflate systemic problems with individual performance and encourage harmful local optimization.
